# Congo War Security Review
## An in-depth review of activity related to the war in the eastern DRC between M23 and its Rwandan backers and pro-Congolese government forces every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Map shapefiles are available by request via email at criticalthreats@aei.org.expand
[
Africa Team Lead
Liam Karr
](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/liam-karr) [
Africa Analyst
Yale Ford
](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/yale-ford)

##  Congo War Security Review, March 30, 2026 
](https://www.criticalthreats.org/briefs/congo-war-security-review/march-30-2026) Authors:   
[Claire Schreder](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/claire-schreder), [Yale Ford](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/yale-ford)   

[ 1 day ago ](https://www.criticalthreats.org/briefs/congo-war-security-review/march-30-2026)
Data Cutoff: March 30, 2026, at 1 P.M. EST
 **To receive the Africa File and Congo War Security Review via email, please subscribe**[ **here**](https://info.aei.org/subscribe-to-africa-file) **.**   
 **Follow CTP on** [**X**](https://x.com/criticalthreats) **,** [**LinkedIn**](https://www.linkedin.com/company/critical-threats-project/posts/?feedView=all) **, and** [**BlueSky**](https://bsky.app/profile/criticalthreats.bsky.social) **for daily updates.**   
 **Read CTP’s latest analysis on the war in the eastern DRC in the weekly Africa File**[ **here**](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/is-morocco-spain-drc-m23-somalia-fgs-southwest-sudan-rsf-ethiopia-sudan-chad-rsf-drc-uganda-iscap-africa-file-march-26-2026#DRC-M23) **.**    
 **Key Takeaway:** _The Congolese army announced imminent military operations under its obligations to the Washington Peace Accords. The Doha process will reportedly relocate to Switzerland in April. M23 withdrew from additional positions in Lubero district in North Kivu. M23 clashed with pro-Congolese government Wazalendo fighters in southern Masisi district in North Kivu._
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260330-DRC-Main-Map-v2.png)
 **Kinetic Activity in M23-Controlled Areas   **  
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Northern Axis: Lubero-Butembo-Beni-Bunia**   
M23 withdrew from additional positions in Lubero district in North Kivu. Pro-Congolese government journalists reported on social media that M23 forces withdrew from Pitakongo and Bunyatenge villages in Lubero on March 28.[1] CTP is unable to verify the location of Pitakongo. Bunyatenge is a remote gold mining village approximately 17 miles west of Bingi in southern Lubero on a local road. CTP reported previously that M23 withdrew from at least a dozen positions near the front line in northern Lubero between March 23 and 24.[2] Officials installed by M23 under a parallel administration reportedly left these areas, and Wazalendo fighters have reoccupied numerous villages in recent days.[3]
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260330-DRC-Northern-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Southern Axis: Bukavu-Uvira-Baraka-Kalemie**
M23 claimed that the Congolese army (FARDC) and allied forces conducted drone attacks on M23-aligned militia fighters in the South Kivu highlands. M23 spokesperson Lawrence Kanyuka claimed in a statement on social media on March 30 that the FARDC and allied forces conducted attacks using one-way attack (OWA) drones on M23-aligned Twirwaneho fighters in two villages in the highlands around Minembwe, including Mikenke—about 15 miles north of Minembwe on the RP527—between March 29 and March 30.[4] M23 and M23-aligned media sources had claimed that the FARDC conducted previous OWA drone attacks on Twirwaneho positions in Mikenge and around Minembwe town between March 24 and 27.[5]
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260327-DRC-Southern-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Northwest Axis: Masisi-Walikale-Lubutu-Kisangani**  
M23 clashed with pro-Congolese government Wazalendo fighters in southern Masisi district in North Kivu. The pro-Congolese government media outlet _Tazama RDC_ reported that M23 clashed with Wazalendo fighters and recaptured two villages in the Katoyi sector, including Mitimingi, in southern Masisi around March 27.[6] Mitimingi is in a remote area in Katoyi, about 26 miles southwest of Rubaya on local roads.
M23 recaptured two villages southwest of Masisi town. M23 reportedly attacked Wazalendo fighters and recaptured three villages in the Nyamaboko group, including Mukohwa, on March 29.[7] Mukohwa is about 18 miles southwest of Masisi on a local road, near the border with Walikale district. The FARDC and Wazalendo fighters reportedly repelled separate M23 attacks on their positions in Ngululu and Kazinga villages, the latter of which is about five miles northwest of Mukohwa toward Masisi town, on March 29.[8] The FARDC and Wazalendo fighters had recaptured Kazinga from M23 in early March.[9]
Fighting between M23 and Wazalendo fighters also took place north of the RP1030 in Walikale district in North Kivu. Wazalendo fighters reportedly retook control of Mpeti village on the RP1030 in Walikale on March 29, after M23 had withdrawn from the area on March 26.[10] The Congolese media outlet  _Les Volcans_ reported that M23 repelled a Wazalendo attack in Ihula village in the Kisimba group in Walikale district in North Kivu on March 28.[11] Ihula is in a remote area north of Kalembe on the RP1030 and about 19 miles east of Mpeti. M23 reportedly retook control of two villages that they had withdrawn from under Wazalendo military pressure in the Ikobo group, which is north of Kisimba—including Kanune, west of Kanyabayonga in Lubero district and about 15 miles northeast of Mpeti in a remote forested area—on March 28.[12]
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260330-DRC-Northwest-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Southwest Axis: Bukavu-Kamituga-Shabunda-Kindu**  
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Major Political Developments**   
The FARDC announced imminent military operations under its obligations to the Washington Peace Accords. General Jacques Ychaligonza, the FARDC Deputy Chief of Staff in charge of operations and intelligence, said on March 29 that he deployed to Kisangani in the central DRC to launch the start of operations against the the Forces démocratiques de libération du Rwanda (FDLR).[13] Ychaligonza said that the FDLR must disarm “whether willingly or by force.”[14] Ychaligonza said that the FARDC mobilized three battalions for  operations to track down and neutralize FDLR militants.[15] Ychaligonza said that the FARDC would intern the FDLR fighters in a military camp in Kisangani in the central DRC as a result of successful operations, before repatriating them to Rwanda.[16]
The Doha process will reportedly relocate to Switzerland in April. The Congolese government and M23 will reportedly resume peace talks in Switzerland under the framework of the Doha process in mid-April.[17] The Congolese foreign minister had said on March 25 that the process has been “paralyzed” in part due to constraints on Qatar’s ability to host the talks amid the war in the Middle East.[18]
 **Significant US-Backed Regional Economic Activity**
Nothing significant to report.

##  Congo War Security Review, March 27, 2026 
](https://www.criticalthreats.org/briefs/congo-war-security-review/march-27-2026) Authors:   
[Claire Schreder](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/claire-schreder), [Yale Ford](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/yale-ford)   

[ 4 days ago ](https://www.criticalthreats.org/briefs/congo-war-security-review/march-27-2026)
Data Cutoff: March 27, 2026, at 1 P.M. EST
 **To receive the Africa File and Congo War Security Review via email, please subscribe**[ **here**](https://info.aei.org/subscribe-to-africa-file) **.**   
 **Follow CTP on** [**X**](https://x.com/criticalthreats) **,** [**LinkedIn**](https://www.linkedin.com/company/critical-threats-project/posts/?feedView=all) **, and** [**BlueSky**](https://bsky.app/profile/criticalthreats.bsky.social) **for daily updates.**   
 **Read CTP’s assessment on the restart of the Washington Peace Accords in**[ **Deadlock in the DRC: Africa File Special Edition**](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/deadlock-in-the-drc-rwanda-m23-fdlr-doha-africa-file) **.**
 **Read CTP’s latest analysis on the war in the eastern DRC in the weekly Africa File**[ **here**](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/is-morocco-spain-drc-m23-somalia-fgs-southwest-sudan-rsf-ethiopia-sudan-chad-rsf-drc-uganda-iscap-africa-file-march-26-2026#DRC-M23) **.**    
 **Key Takeaway:** _The United States reiterated its call for the DRC and Rwanda to immediately implement their peace commitments during a United Nations Security Council meeting. M23 reportedly withdrew unilaterally from its forward positions in Walikale district in North Kivu. The Congolese army and allied forces reportedly conducted air attacks on M23-aligned militia fighters in the South Kivu highlands. Pro-Congolese government Wazalendo fighters attacked M23 south of Ruthsuru town in Rutshuru district in North Kivu._
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260327-DRC-Main-Map-v2.png)
 **Kinetic Activity in M23-Controlled Areas   **  
Pro-Congolese government Wazalendo fighters attacked M23 south of Ruthsuru town in Rutshuru district in North Kivu. Wazalendo fighters reportedly attacked M23 elements on patrol near Kalengera village in the Bwisha chiefdom in Rutshuru on March 26.[1] Kalengera is about six miles south of Rutshuru town on the RN2, on the eastern edge of the Virunga National Park.
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260327-DRC-Rutshuru-Map-v2.png)
 **Northern Axis: Lubero-Butembo-Beni-Bunia**   
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Southern Axis: Bukavu-Uvira-Baraka-Kalemie**
The Congolese army (FARDC) and allied forces reportedly conducted air attacks on M23-aligned militia fighters in the South Kivu highlands. M23 and M23-aligned media sources claimed that the FARDC and allied forces conducted several air attacks using one-way attack drones on M23-aligned Twirwaneho positions around Minembwe town and in Mikenge—about 15 miles north of Minembwe on the RP527—between March 24 and 27.[2] One attack allegedly targeted a community radio station near Minembwe center on March 25.[3] M23 and Twirwaneho denounced the attacks in several statements between March 24 and 27.[4]
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260327-DRC-Southern-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Northwest Axis: Masisi-Walikale-Lubutu-Kisangani**  
M23 reportedly withdrew unilaterally from its forward positions in Walikale district in North Kivu. M23 reportedly withdrew its forces and equipment from Minjenje and Mpeti villages in the Kisimba group in Walikale district on March 26.[5] Minjenje is an important logistic point for M23, located two miles southeast of Mpeti, which is the group’s westernmost position on the RP1030 about 12 miles east of Pinga. The Congolese media outlet _Actualite.cd_ reported that M23 also withdrew its forces from Kanune village—15 miles northeast of Mpeti in a remote forested area in the Ikobo group in Walikale on March 26.[6] Wazalendo fighters had previously attacked M23 in Kanune on March 22.[7] Wazalendo fighters reportedly clashed with M23 in a different area in Ikobo and recaptured Kateku village on March 26.[8] CTP is unable to verify the location of Kateku.
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260327-DRC-Northwest-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Southwest Axis: Bukavu-Kamituga-Shabunda-Kindu**  
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Major Political Developments**   
The United States reiterated its call for the DRC and Rwanda to immediately implement their peace commitments during a United Nations Security Council (UNSC) meeting. Massad Boulos, US President Donald Trump’s Africa adviser, chaired a UNSC meeting on the eastern DRC crisis and reaffirmed US support and engagement for the Washington and Doha processes on March 26.[9] Boulos called for the “immediate withdrawal” of Rwandan troops from the eastern DRC and for the FARDC to immediately neutralize the Forces démocratiques de libération du Rwanda (FDLR) in areas under its control as part of the US-brokered peace agreement from June 2025.[10] Boulos reaffirmed that the United States will “explore all available tools” to ensure the long-term success of the peace accords, implying the use of sanctions.[11] Vivian van de Perre, the acting head of the UN force in the DRC, warned in her statement that the situation in the eastern DRC is deteriorating amid continued fighting, citing a widening gap between diplomatic efforts and conditions on the ground.[12]
 **Significant US-Backed Regional Economic Activity**
Nothing significant to report.

[
##  Congo War Security Review, March 25, 2026 
](https://www.criticalthreats.org/briefs/congo-war-security-review/march-25-2026) Authors:   
[Yale Ford](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/yale-ford), [Claire Schreder](https://www.criticalthreats.org/team/claire-schreder)   

[ March 25, 2026 ](https://www.criticalthreats.org/briefs/congo-war-security-review/march-25-2026)
Data Cutoff: March 25, 2026, at 1 P.M. EST
 **To receive the Africa File and Congo War Security Review via email, please subscribe**[ **here**](https://info.aei.org/subscribe-to-africa-file) **.**   
 **Follow CTP on** [**X**](https://x.com/criticalthreats) **,** [**LinkedIn**](https://www.linkedin.com/company/critical-threats-project/posts/?feedView=all) **, and** [**BlueSky**](https://bsky.app/profile/criticalthreats.bsky.social) **for daily updates.**   
 **Read CTP’s assessment on the restart of the Washington Peace Accords in**[ **Deadlock in the DRC: Africa File Special Edition**](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/deadlock-in-the-drc-rwanda-m23-fdlr-doha-africa-file) **.**
 **Read CTP’s latest analysis on the war in the eastern DRC in the weekly Africa File**[ **here**](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/sudan-rsf-saf-sahel-drc-iscap-uganda-nigeria-boko-iswap-mali-fla-jnim-africa-file-march-26-2026#DRC) **.**    
 **Key Takeaway:** _M23 withdrew from its forward positions in Lubero district in northern North Kivu. Pro-Congolese government forces repelled an M23 attack along the RP529 in Walikale district in North Kivu._
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260325-DRC-Main-Map-v2.png)
 **Kinetic Activity in M23-Controlled Areas   **  
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Northern Axis: Lubero-Butembo-Beni-Bunia**   
M23 withdrew from its forward positions in Lubero district in northern North Kivu. M23 reportedly withdrew its forces and military equipment unilaterally from at least a dozen positions near the front line in northern Lubero between March 23 and 24.[1] This included Kipese, which is about eight miles southeast of Lubero town on a local road. Security sources told the Congolese media outlet _Radio Okapi_ on March 25 that M23 maintained its forward position in Katondi, roughly 10 miles south of Lubero town on the RN2, however.[2] M23 forces reportedly headed southward after withdrawing.[3] M23 had advanced to capture areas in northern Lubero in February and March 2025, before Ugandan army troops blocked further advances and established a de facto buffer zone.[4]
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260325-DRC-Northern-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Southern Axis: Bukavu-Uvira-Baraka-Kalemie**
Nothing significant to report.
 **Northwest Axis: Masisi-Walikale-Lubutu-Kisangani**  
Pro-Congolese government forces repelled an M23 attack along the RP529 in Walikale district in North Kivu. The Congolese media outlet _Actualite.cd_ reported that Wazalendo fighters thwarted an M23 attempt to flank Congolese army and pro-Congolese government Wazalendo positions on the RP529 in Walikale district and attack Mungazi village on March 23.[5] Mungazi is about seven miles west of M23’s forward position on the RP529 in Kibati village and 21 miles west of Nyabiondo town in Masisi district.
![](https://www.criticalthreats.org/wp-content/uploads/20260325-DRC-Northwest-Axis-Map-v2.png)
 **Southwest Axis: Bukavu-Kamituga-Shabunda-Kindu**  
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Major Political Developments**   
Nothing significant to report.  
 **Significant US-Backed Regional Economic Activity**
Nothing significant to report.
